stem: The linear polynomial \( P(x) \) passes through the points \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) and \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \). What is \( P([[xe]]) \)?
solution: The line through the two points has slope \( \dfrac{y_1-y_0}{x_1-x_0} \), so \( P(x)=y_0+\dfrac{y_1-y_0}{x_1-x_0}(x-x_0) \); at \( x=[[xe]] \) this gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This anchors the line at \( (x_1,y_1) \) but still measures displacement from \( x_0 \), double counting the offset.
This flips the sign of the slope.
This uses the wrong denominator, measuring the run to \( x_1 \) from \( x_e \) instead of from \( x_0 \).
This just averages the two \( y \)-values, ignoring where \( x_e \) actually falls.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The quadratic polynomial \( P(x) \) passes through \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \), and \( ([[x2]], [[y2]]) \). Using Lagrange interpolation, find \( P([[xe]]) \).
solution: The Lagrange form is \( P(x)=\sum_i y_i \ell_i(x) \) with \( \ell_i(x)=\prod_{j\ne i}\frac{x-x_j}{x_i-x_j} \); evaluating at \( x=[[xe]] \) gives \( P([[xe]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This just averages the three \( y \)-values, ignoring where \( x_e \) actually falls.
This drops the third Lagrange basis term entirely.
This flips the sign of the middle Lagrange basis term.
This just adds the three \( y \)-values, unrelated to the interpolated value.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: For interpolation nodes \( x_0=[[x0]] \), \( x_1=[[x1]] \), \( x_2=[[x2]] \), the Lagrange basis polynomial associated with \( x_1 \) is \( \ell_1(x) = \dfrac{(x-x_0)(x-x_2)}{(x_1-x_0)(x_1-x_2)} \). What is \( \ell_1([[xe]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is actually the basis polynomial for node \( x_0 \), not \( x_1 \).
This uses the wrong second factor in the numerator (\(x-x_1\) instead of \(x-x_2\)).
This uses the wrong sign convention in the denominator's first factor.
This is the value \( \ell_1 \) takes AT its own node \( x_1 \) (always 1), not at \( x_e \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The quadratic polynomial \( P(x) = ax^2+bx+c \) passes through \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \), and \( ([[x2]], [[y2]]) \). Using Newton's divided differences, find the leading coefficient \( a \).
solution: The leading coefficient is the second-order divided difference \( f[x_0,x_1,x_2] = \dfrac{f[x_1,x_2]-f[x_0,x_1]}{x_2-x_0} \), which evaluates to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the first divided difference \( f[x_0,x_1]\), the LINEAR slope, not the quadratic leading coefficient.
This forgets to divide by \( x_2-x_0 \) at the end.
This adds the two first-order divided differences instead of subtracting them.
This subtracts the two first-order divided differences in the wrong order, flipping the sign.
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.
